1: et: The first word invites readers of the poems in sequence to connect 3.8 to 3.7, but poetic self-consciousness, all but invisible in the previous poem, is on immediate display here when the very value of poetic song is questioned. suspicit: admire (OLD suspicio 2)
2: An indirect statement with carmen as an accusative subject. Sense rather than syntax prohibits dotes from being the accusative subject. dotes = value (OLD dos 4), but the primary association with a dowry may call to mind a form of courtship.
3: The speaker contrasts cultural values then (quondam) and now (nunc, 4) in this couplet.
4: barbaria = a “barbarism” (i.e., characteristic of uncivilized or uncultured people) habere: subject infinitive with nihil as its direct object. #IIIF The editor of the 1916 Teubner adds a comma after grandis, presumably to set off the subject infinitive and make the reading easier. Does the punctuation help?
5: cum: an adversative translation like “although” or “even when” works well here #IIIF The orthography used by the primary scribe of Manuscript P makes the text ambiguous here, but it has a minimal impact on the meaning. Sometimes the scribe will indicate the diphthong “ae” by writing an ewith a tiny marking at its base, known as a e-caudata (“tailed e”). It look similar to ę, but with the serif shooting off in a southwesterly direction; a good example appears in 3.7.49 with fortunę (#IIIF), but there isn’t much consistency observed. There may be a hint of one such serif on pulchre, and another on domine, suggesting that we would write these instead as pulchrae and dominae in our present-day orthography. Many editors prefer to print pulchre for pulchrae and read it as an adverb.
6: quo = “to which place” licet and licuit are both impersonal. You might place the words of this verse in English word order as follows: non licet mihi ire quo licuit libris (ire). The speaker presents himself as separate from his books; they are accepted into his mistress’s company, but he, the exclusus amator, is not. #bookmateriality
7: cum: another adversative translation works here. me or libros is the implied object of laudauit, but the speaker leaves it ambiguous. laudato = a substantive participle referring to the speaker. Now the speaker suggests that he is the one praised, even though she may have only praised his books of poetry.
8: The tension between turpiter and ingeniosus seems to main point of this verse.
9: The speaker supplies an explanation for his exclusion: his mistress has taken up with a wealthy rival. dives = “a rich man” (substantive) parto … censu: ablative absolute
10: pastus = “having grown fat” (cf. OLD pasco 3b), but there word consistently has a sense of the animalian to it, especially when placed next to eques, so closely resembling equus, here. With ecce opening the couplet, a surprising birth of sorts is described: a miraculous monster—raised on and enriched by blood—has appeared in the speaker’s life and poem.
11: hunc gains emphasis through its appearance at the beginning of the verse. Cf huius in the next verse, too. amplecti: a complementary infinitive (deponent verb) uita: vocative (repeated in the following verse)
12: Note how the syntax parallels that of the previous verse.
13: caput hoc: The speaker addresses himself to his mistress and describes features of the eques, his rival, as if he stands before them.
14: latus: antecedent of quod. The relative clause separates the two components of the verb cinctum … est. The speaker uses graphic word order as he proceeds from his rival’s head down his body to his flank, girded with the sword at the beginning of the verse, to his left hand (v. 15), also on the left-hand side of the verse.
15: aurum: Equites (pl. of eques, v. 10) were allowed to wear a gold ring (annulus aureus) on the left hand. nunc serum emphasizes how recently the eques has gained his ring and status.
16: scuta: the plural provides a subtle sense of the frequency with which the man armed himself for battle. It also reminds the mistress that the rival didn’t fight as an equestrian; the scutum was conventionally carried by heavy infantry (OLD scutum), as opposed to the parma dextram (sc. manum): the right hand would have wielded the death-dealing gladius and thus have been more prone to being covered in gore. tange: the speaker is still addressing his mistress.
17: qua: look to the main clause that follows for the antecedent (or “postcedent”) of this relative pronoun.
18: est is implied as the main verb #IIIF Manuscript S for the better reading of qua periit vs quae perit in P (92verso).
20: illi: dative (of reference / advantage) The clause quicquid habet functions as the subject of quaesitum est
21: iugulauerit: subjunctive in an indirect question. A more graphic form of bloodshed that typically involved killing by cutting the throat.
22: indicet: the speaker hypothesizes that his rival will brag about how many times he shed blood. hoc: the object of the participle fassas, which modifies the accusative feminine manūs (as the scansion implies). You could render this as “hands having confessed [to] this” (i.e., the frequency of murder). auara: the bloodthirst ascribed to the rival is paralleled by the mistress’s own acquisitiveness.
23: purus: the speaker emphasizes his purity. (Cf. the implicit purity of the priestess (sacerdos) in 3.7.21-22.)
24: inane: the song’s ineffectual nature is confirmed by the speaker’s ongoing exclusion.
25: The speaker encourages savvy students to study something other than (amatory) poetry. discite: a new (plural) audience is addressed. sapitis: the verb sapiō places greater emphasis on discernment and good taste than on simple knowledge. quae: the antecedent has been attracted into the relative pronoun. (E.g., “I have WHAT you want.” vs. “I have the things that you want.”) If you want to create an antecedent, imagine an ea as a direct object of discite. nos … inertes: the adjective iners (< in+ars) is in ironic tension with artes from v. 1. Here it may also connote uselessness, powerlessness, or even sexual frustration (OLD iners 5d)
26: sequi: a (deponent) complementary infinitive with discite. (A&G 456). Note the variation in construction between this and the previous verse.
27: deducite: a pun on two different senses of deducere, one of which relates to drawing a weapon, the other of which metaphorically refers to finely spinning thread and, by extension, producing highly refined literature. For a famous Ovidian use of the latter sense, see Metamorphoses 1.4: deducite … carmen.
28: nox tibi, si belles: #IIIF an emendation of hic tibi si uelles. Mini lesson on textual criticism? Reasons for emendation = sequence of tenses? pronunciation of uelles vs belles? Was Homer famously chaste? nox = “a night’s lovemaking” (OLD nox 3c) (emendation by ) Cf. noctes dari in Pl. Truculentus 279:   amabit sarculum,    ruri amplexari mavelim patulam bovem  ea noctem  stramentis pernoctare perpetem,  tuas  cenatas   dono dari.
29: The next three couplets offer a variant version of the story of Danae, a princess in Argos and daughter of King Acrisius. A more conventional version is as follows: Acrisius, after consulting an oracle and learning that Danae’s future son would kill him, locked his daughter in a tower (or, in earlier versions, hid her away in a subterranean chamber) to prevent her from ever conceiving a child. Zeus/Jupiter, smitten with Danae, was undeterred by the tower and, having appeared to her as a stream or shower of gold, impregnated her. (Literary accounts do not emphasize or acknowledge her agency, but depictions of the encounter on —not to mention —often portray her as welcoming the gold.) In one extant version of the myth, her son Perseus, famed for slaying the gorgon, would go on to kill Acrisius accidentally with an errant discus. (Pseudo-Hyginus Fabulae 63) The speaker’s version here presents a uirgo (Danae) whom Jupiter is wooing and whose father’s reluctance is chalked up to the suitor’s lack of money. When Jupiter appears in the form of gold itself, the maiden offers herself to her lover. admonitus: introduces an indirect statement. The passive construction provides no insight into who offered Jupiter this advice.
30: corruptae … uirginis: the censorious tone of the participle seems to align with the speaker’s disappointment in what he views as his own mistress’s susceptibility to the allures of money embodied in the figure of the eques.
32: erat (and erant) can be redistributed for all the noun/adjective pairs in this couplet.
33: sapiens: Because of the word order, consider translating this adjective adverbially. in munere = “in the form of the gift” or “as the gift” (OLD in 36d)
34: sinus: scansion shows this to be accusative plural. Translate as “embraces” (OLD sinus 2b).
35: The Age of Saturn, often—and rather surprisingly—referred to as the Golden Age, was a time when heavy metals like gold remained unearthed; money was unknown; the earth’s natural production rendered farming and private property unnecessary; and sailing and trade were nonexistent. It is often invoked as a time of simple, peaceful living. regna: poetic plural suggesting the “powers” of kingship
37: aera: from aes, aeris, n. = copper, bronze, or money in general.
38: admorat: a rare, syncopated form of admouerat, appearing only here in extant Latin. The verb’s implied subject is Saturnus (v. 35).
39: sine: surrounded by the two ablatives it governs fruges: an accusative (along with poma and mella in v. 40) acting as a direct object in apposition with meliora.
40: cavā: scansion shows this to be ablative.
41: Note the graphic word order. scindebat: the verb construes plowing as an act of violence.
43: freta: a poetic word for the sea. Sea travel, associated with trade and warfare, marked a departure from the Golden Age. (Cf. the Nurse’s opening speech in Medea.)
44: mortali: a substantive adjective acting as a dative of reference.
45: The speaker addresses human nature (hominum natura) directly. sollers: often paired with ad to indicate in what respect someone was skilled or clever. contra emphasizes that the cleverness was not to human nature’s ultimate benefit.
46: ingeniosa: the speaker uses the same adjective to describe himself in v. 8.
47: quo: “to what end/purpose” or “why,” repeated in the next verse and varied with quid in v. 49. Cf. 3.7.19. tibi: the speaker continues to address human nature in the singular. incingere: perhaps a subject infinitive with a finite form of esse implied. Taken with quo tibi, the sense is akin to a second person verb: “to what end do you encircle…”
48: For quo tibi … addere, see previous note. addere in = “to bring near to.” Interpret discordes … manus as the direct object of addere and arma as the object of the preposition in. Cf. adde manus in vincla meas, Am. 1.7.1.
49: The speaker returns to the topic of seafaring. quid tibi cum: a finite form of esse is implied. The sense is “What are you doing with…?” Cf. Cic. Cael. 33 quid tibi cum Caelio? terrā: scansion shows this to be ablative contenta: a nominative (as shown by scansion) that modifies natura fuisses: pluperfect subjunctive expressing an unrealized or unfulfilled wish (optative) for the past.
50: cur non…?: a question laced with irony. regna: poetic plural, as in v. 35
51: The speaker concedes that human nature has already made some ventures into the sky. adfectas: this verb may connote a sense of overweening ambition. Cf. Amores 1.1.14. templa: a fundamental element in the creation of certain temples or sanctuaries was an augur’s delineation of a specific space in the air from which future auguries could be taken. Cf. Servius on Verg. Aen. 1.92: templum dicitur locus manu auguris designates in aëre, post quem factum ilico captantur auguria. Quirinus: the name attributed to Romulus after his deification.
52: Liber: an archaic Latin deity associated with planting and the propagation of fruite who came to be identified with Bacchus, god of wine. Was he initially a mortal? #check Alcides: Hercules was the most famous descendent of his grandfather Alceus, so this patronymic refers to him. Caesar: a temple to Julius Caesar (Aedes Divi Iuli) was dedicated in the Roman forum in 29 BCE, 15 years after his assassination.
55: curia: the Senate-house, but here referring to membership in the senate. census: the process by which citizens and their property were registered and classified. honores: perhaps simple “honors” but also “privileges,” including the opportunity to pursue certain public offices (i.e., those in the cursus honorum).
56: inde = “from there” (i.e., by the census). The speaker notes a specific civic role and status determined by the census. iudex: after Julius Caesar passed the Lex Iulia iudiciorum publicorum in 46 BCE, only private individuals from higher social classes (senatorial and equestrian) could preside as judges in Roman civil hearings. tough eques: under Augustus, status as an eques could be conferred upon any citizen who met a property requirement of 100,000 denari as determined through a census.
57: Two couplets populated primarily by independent verbs in the subjunctive. Read them jussively with equites and senators as the implied subjects. Campus = the Campus Martius, a place of official assembly for the Roman people that was also used for military exercises and recreation.
59: liceantur: the speaker imagines that his wealthier rivals appraise his love interests like property to be bid upon.
60: satis est: a parenthetical, the force of which seems to fall on aliquid. pauperis: partitive genitive with aliquid
61: licet + subjunctive verb = “although” or “even if” Sabina: a foundational episode in Roman self-conception, the “rape of the Sabine women” entailed the forced abduction—and implicit sexual assault—of young women from the Sabine population by early Roman men following a plan devised by Romulus. After becoming the first “Roman women,” they interceded in a subsequent battle between their (Roman) husbands and (Sabine) fathers to prevent further bloodshed. That the speaker thinks his mistress resembles “gloomy” (taetricas) Sabine women implies both that she seems appealingly upset (whatever that may mean) and that the eques is akin to an abductor.
62: imperat: his rival, the eques, is the implied subject. His wealth makes him imperious, as the speaker views the matter. ut + indicative = “as” qui: the antecedent has been attracted into the relative clause, but you can think of it as the noun uir (or even miles) after ut.
63: The speaker returns to the explicit complaint voiced in vv. 5-6: his mistress will not see him. in me = “on my account” The repetition of me shows the speaker’s incredulity that he is singled out for this treatment. maritum: the speaker reveals his mistress is married
64: si dederim: the implied object is “gifts” (dona). If only he had the resources to provide her with material gifts, the speaker hypothesizes, the woman would see to it that her guard was less vigilant, and she would forget all fear of her husband. Cf. dederis in Am. 3.4.48. An alternative reading (e.g., Hutchinson 2008: 105n12) has the husband tacitly condoning the affair, thus flouting one of the specific prohibitions of Augustus’ Lex Iulia de adulteriis coercendis.
